Reason: Foundational local and CI workflow for sandbox control and troubleshooting.

Reason: Supports repeatable state setup and fast rollback during tests and demos.

Reason: Lets custom automation share the same XRPL environment without manual client wiring.

Reason: Rounds out common payment-related transaction helpers.

Reason: Provides access to streaming-payment style workflows in the same tool.
Notes: channel create / fund / claim / sign / verify / list

Reason: Needed for account flags, signer lists, and account configuration flows.
Notes: accountset set / clear / signer-list / info

Reason: Supports newer transaction sequencing patterns developers may need to test.

Reason: Useful for authorization-based account interaction scenarios.
Notes: depositpreauth authorize / unauthorize / list

Reason: Makes the same workflows usable across local and shared XRPL test environments.
Notes: local / testnet / devnet

Reason: Likely better as a separate project because load generation and workload control have a different runtime model and scope than the sandbox tool's developer CLI.
Notes: Starts the workload API server for traffic generation.

Reason: Useful after the Phase 1 local sandbox baseline, when the focus expands to local multi-node network workflows.
Notes: Generates and runs config for a local multi-node network from config.toml.

Reason: Likely better as a separate project because transaction load generation has a different runtime model and scope than the sandbox tool's developer CLI.
Notes: Sends weighted XRPL transaction mixes for real-usage simulation and load tests.

Reason: Likely better as a separate project because transaction lifecycle tracking and live workload management make the sandbox heavier and less developer-focused.
Notes: Tracks CREATED -> SUBMITTED -> VALIDATED/REJECTED/EXPIRED/FAILED_NET and retries retryable submissions.

Reason: Useful after the lightweight sandbox baseline, when the focus expands to richer visibility and diagnostics for more advanced workflows.
Notes: Live metrics UI with counts by state/type, success rates, diagnostics, and drill-down tables.

Reason: Likely better as a separate project because workload validation infrastructure adds runtime complexity beyond a lightweight developer sandbox.
Notes: Subscribes to ledger/accounts_proposed/transactions, uses event queue + reconnect, and falls back to RPC polling.

Reason: Likely better as a separate project because persistent workload state and resume behavior are not part of a lightweight sandbox's core role.
Notes: Persists wallets, transactions, validations, and balances for restart/resume.

Reason: Likely better as a separate project because assertion/event instrumentation belongs with workload execution rather than the base sandbox CLI.
Notes: Optional assertion and event-tracing hooks with local fallback behavior.

Reason: Likely better as a separate project because automated recovery logic is part of workload orchestration, not lightweight sandbox management.
Notes: Expires stale transactions, invalidates fee cache on telINSUF_FEE_P, and auto-creates accounts during workload.

Reason: Likely better as a separate project because these settings mainly configure workload generation and runtime behavior rather than the lightweight sandbox tool itself.
Notes: Runtime configuration via RPC_URL, WS_URL, RIPPLED_IP, WORKLOAD_PERSIST, TESTNET_DIR, GEN_BIN, TESTNET_VALIDATORS, LOG_LEVEL.

Reason: Likely better as a separate project because transaction load generation has a different runtime model and scope than the sandbox tool's developer CLI.
Notes: Structured config for funding, gateways, users, AMM, currencies, transactions, timeouts, genesis, and rippled settings.

Reason: Useful in a later phase when the sandbox tool expands from a lightweight sandbox into fuller local network generation, because this command creates an end-to-end runnable environment rather than only the ledger data.
Notes: Full environment generation: ledger.json, accounts.json, per-validator xrpld.cfg, and docker-compose.yml.

Reason: Useful in a later phase when ledger-generation workflows become part of the sandbox tool, because this command focuses on producing genesis ledger data without the rest of the runnable network setup.
Notes: Ledger-only generation for ledger.json and accounts.json with trustline, AMM, MPT, and amendment controls.

Reason: Useful in a later phase when the sandbox tool expands into guided multi-node network setup, because validator config generation is more advanced and harder for typical users to navigate in a lightweight Phase 1 CLI.
Notes: Generates validator and hub xrpld.cfg files from a template.

Reason: Useful in a later phase when the sandbox tool expands into guided multi-node network setup, because compose generation for several nodes introduces more infrastructure detail than a lightweight Phase 1 sandbox should expose directly.
Notes: Generates docker-compose.yml for validators and hub nodes.

Reason: Useful in a later phase when the sandbox tool expands into fuller generated-network configuration, because profile-driven amendment setup is more advanced than the lightweight Phase 1 sandbox baseline.
Notes: Supports mainnet, develop, and custom amendment profiles plus per-amendment overrides.

Reason: Useful in a later phase if the sandbox tool adds more advanced genesis or local network generation flows, because these compact specs are mainly shorthand inputs for generated starting-state configuration.
Notes: Trustline, AMM pool, and MPT issuance compact string specs for generation input.

Reason: Likely better as a separate project or advanced module because a programmable generation API is broader than the lightweight sandbox tool's core CLI scope.
Notes: Importable generator API with LedgerConfig, AccountConfig, FeeConfig, and related config classes.

Reason: Useful in a later phase when larger network generation and faster account/key creation become important, but not necessary for the lightweight Phase 1 sandbox baseline.
Notes: CPU, fast native, and GPU-accelerated account/key generation paths.

Reason: Useful in a later phase for CI and advanced generator automation, because it lets generation settings be supplied through GL_* environment variables instead of only CLI flags or config files.
Notes: All config fields support GL_* environment variables and .env loading.
